seo.title: Government Research Agents AI Agent Checklist
seo.description: Production readiness checklist for research agents AI agents in government. Covers FedRAMP, FISMA, NIST 800-53.

content.intro: Deploying AI research agents in government contexts carries extraordinary stakes: a single misconfiguration can expose classified data, produce biased policy analysis, or generate legally indefensible decisions affecting millions of citizens. Federal agencies must satisfy overlapping mandates from FedRAMP, FISMA, NIST 800-53, and the Executive Order on AI before any agent touches production workflows. This checklist provides concrete, auditable steps to ensure your multi-step web search and synthesis agents meet the rigorous standards required for government deployment.

sections.heading: Data Classification & Access Controls
sections.description: Government research agents must enforce strict data boundaries to prevent classified or sensitive information from being processed by unauthorized systems, including external LLMs. Every data pathway must be inventoried, classified, and gated before agent deployment.

items.label: Classify all data sources before agent ingestion
items.description: Audit every web source, internal database, and API the agent will query. Tag each source with a classification level (Unclassified, CUI, Secret, Top Secret) and block the agent from querying any source above its authorized clearance level.

items.label: Enforce hard block on classified data routing to external LLMs
items.description: Implement a pre-processing filter that scans all input data for classification markers, CUI designations, and PII before forwarding to any external model API. Terminate the request and log an alert if classified content is detected.

items.label: Implement role-based access control (RBAC) aligned to NIST 800-53 AC-2
items.description: Define agent access roles mapped to specific research tasks. Require multi-factor authentication for any human operator who can modify agent search parameters, approve outputs, or access raw retrieved data.

items.label: Deploy data loss prevention (DLP) scanning on all agent outputs
items.description: Run every synthesized report through an automated DLP tool configured with government-specific patterns (SSNs, EINs, clearance identifiers, treaty references) before the output is stored or shared.

items.label: Maintain a real-time data lineage log for all retrieved content
items.description: Record the URL, retrieval timestamp, classification tag, and hash of every document the agent ingests during a research session. Store logs in a FedRAMP-authorized storage service with immutable write-once settings.

items.label: Restrict agent network egress to an approved domain allowlist
items.description: Configure network policies so the agent can only query pre-approved government, academic, and vetted public domains. Block all other outbound requests and log any blocked attempt for security review.

items.label: Conduct quarterly access reviews of agent service accounts
items.description: Review all API keys, OAuth tokens, and service account permissions used by the agent every 90 days. Revoke any credentials that exceed least-privilege requirements per NIST 800-53 AC-6.

sections.heading: Source Verification & Information Integrity
sections.description: Research agents that synthesize information for government policy or analysis must produce outputs that are accurate, current, and free from adversarial manipulation. Each retrieval step must include automated verification checks before content enters the synthesis pipeline.

items.label: Validate source authority and domain credibility before ingestion
items.description: Implement a source scoring module that checks each retrieved URL against a curated government-approved source registry (.gov, .edu, peer-reviewed journals, recognized think tanks). Flag and quarantine content from unrecognized or low-trust domains for human review.

items.label: Cross-reference all factual claims against at least two independent sources
items.description: Configure the synthesis agent to require a minimum of two corroborating sources for any factual assertion included in the final report. Automatically annotate single-source claims with a confidence warning visible to the reviewing analyst.

items.label: Enforce recency thresholds based on research topic volatility
items.description: Define maximum content age limits per topic category (e.g., 30 days for geopolitical events, 12 months for regulatory data, 5 years for historical analysis). Automatically exclude or flag sources that exceed the threshold for the active research task.

items.label: Run automated bias detection on synthesized summaries
items.description: Apply a bias detection model to each generated summary to identify political framing, demographic skew, or one-sided sourcing. Produce a bias score and source diversity report attached to every research output before analyst review.

items.label: Detect and flag potential adversarial content injection in retrieved pages
items.description: Scan retrieved web content for prompt injection patterns, hidden text, and SEO manipulation techniques before passing content to the LLM synthesis step. Log and discard any page where injection indicators exceed a defined threshold.

items.label: Preserve verbatim source excerpts alongside all synthesized claims
items.description: For every claim in the final report, store the exact source excerpt, source URL, and retrieval timestamp in a linked evidence record. This enables auditors and analysts to verify the agent's reasoning without re-running the search.

items.label: Implement hallucination detection via retrieval-grounded verification
items.description: After synthesis, run a grounding check that maps each sentence in the output back to a specific retrieved passage. Flag any sentence with no retrievable grounding as a potential hallucination and require human analyst sign-off before inclusion.

items.label: Log all search queries issued by the agent for post-hoc audit
items.description: Record every search query, API call, and URL visited during a research session in a tamper-evident audit log. Retain logs for a minimum of three years per NIST 800-53 AU-11 requirements.

sections.heading: Algorithmic Accountability & Explainability
sections.description: Federal regulations and the Executive Order on AI require that AI-assisted decisions affecting citizens be explainable, auditable, and appealable. Research agents contributing to policy analysis or citizen-facing decisions must produce outputs that a non-technical reviewer can interrogate and challenge.

items.label: Complete an Algorithmic Impact Assessment (AIA) before production deployment
items.description: Conduct a formal AIA documenting the agent's intended use, affected populations, potential for disparate impact, and mitigation controls. Submit the AIA for review by your agency's AI governance board and retain it as a compliance artifact.

items.label: Generate a structured reasoning trace for every research output
items.description: Configure the agent to produce a step-by-step reasoning log showing which sources were retrieved, which claims were extracted, how conflicts were resolved, and what confidence levels were assigned. Attach this trace to every deliverable report.

items.label: Implement a human-in-the-loop review gate before outputs influence policy decisions
items.description: Require a qualified analyst to review and digitally sign off on any agent-generated research report before it is used to inform a policy recommendation, budget decision, or citizen-affecting action. Log the reviewer identity and timestamp.

items.label: Provide a plain-language explanation of agent conclusions for non-technical stakeholders
items.description: Automatically generate a plain-language summary (reading level Grade 8 or below) alongside the technical report. This summary must explain what the agent found, how confident it is, and what limitations apply, satisfying Section 508 accessibility requirements.

items.label: Establish a formal appeals and correction process for agent-influenced decisions
items.description: Document and publish a process by which citizens or internal stakeholders can challenge a decision influenced by agent research. Define SLA timelines for review (e.g., 30 days), assign a responsible human reviewer, and log all appeals and outcomes.

items.label: Track and report model version and configuration for every research session
items.description: Record the exact LLM model version, system prompt hash, temperature settings, and retrieval configuration used for each research task. This enables reproducibility audits and ensures that configuration drift is detectable.

items.label: Conduct semi-annual explainability audits with independent reviewers
items.description: Engage an independent technical reviewer every six months to assess whether the agent's reasoning traces are accurate, complete, and sufficient for a non-expert to understand and challenge the agent's conclusions.

sections.heading: FedRAMP, FISMA & Operational Security Compliance
sections.description: Government AI deployments must operate within a FedRAMP-authorized boundary and satisfy FISMA continuous monitoring requirements. Operational security controls must be validated before go-live and maintained through the full system lifecycle.

items.label: Confirm all infrastructure components hold a FedRAMP Authorization to Operate (ATO)
items.description: Verify that every cloud service, API provider, vector database, and LLM inference endpoint used by the agent has a current FedRAMP Moderate or High authorization. Document the ATO status and expiration date for each component in your system security plan.

items.label: Complete a FISMA System Security Plan (SSP) covering the full agent architecture
items.description: Document the agent's system boundary, data flows, interconnections, and all NIST 800-53 control implementations in a formal SSP. Submit for Authorizing Official review and obtain an ATO before processing any government data.

items.label: Implement continuous monitoring per NIST 800-53 CA-7
items.description: Deploy automated security monitoring tools that continuously scan agent infrastructure for vulnerabilities, configuration drift, and anomalous behavior. Generate monthly POA&M updates and report significant findings to the ISSO within 24 hours.

items.label: Encrypt all data in transit and at rest using FIPS 140-2 validated modules
items.description: Ensure all network traffic between agent components uses TLS 1.2 or higher with FIPS 140-2 validated cipher suites. Confirm that all stored research data, logs, and model outputs are encrypted with FIPS-validated encryption at rest.

items.label: Conduct a penetration test of the agent system before initial ATO and annually thereafter
items.description: Engage a FedRAMP-recognized third-party assessment organization (3PAO) to perform penetration testing of the agent's web search interfaces, API endpoints, and data storage components. Remediate all critical and high findings before go-live.

items.label: Implement incident response playbooks specific to AI agent failure modes
items.description: Develop and test incident response procedures for agent-specific scenarios: data exfiltration via prompt injection, hallucinated policy citations used in official documents, and unauthorized access to restricted data sources. Conduct tabletop exercises annually.

items.label: Validate Section 508 accessibility compliance for all agent-generated interfaces and reports
items.description: Test all user-facing dashboards, report templates, and output formats against WCAG 2.1 AA standards and Section 508 requirements. Include screen reader compatibility, keyboard navigation, and sufficient color contrast in acceptance testing.

items.label: Establish a supply chain risk management process for all third-party AI components
items.description: Inventory all third-party models, libraries, and APIs used by the agent. Assess each for foreign ownership risk, vulnerability history, and license compliance per NIST 800-161. Require vendors to provide software bills of materials (SBOMs).

notes.regulation: Executive Order on AI (EO 14110) & OMB M-24-10
notes.note: Federal agencies must complete pre-deployment risk assessments for all rights-impacting and safety-impacting AI use cases, designate a Chief AI Officer, and maintain an inventory of AI systems reported annually to OMB. Research agents that inform policy decisions or citizen-affecting determinations fall within the rights-impacting category and require the highest level of human oversight and documentation.

All federal information systems, including AI agent platforms, must implement the full NIST 800-53 control baseline appropriate to their impact level (Low, Moderate, or High). For research agents handling sensitive government data, a Moderate baseline is the minimum, requiring controls across 20 control families including audit and accountability (AU), access control (AC), and system and communications protection (SC). Annual assessments and continuous monitoring are mandatory.

Any cloud-based component of the agent architecture—including LLM inference APIs, vector databases, and web search APIs—must have a current FedRAMP authorization before processing federal data. Agencies cannot accept a vendor's SOC 2 or ISO 27001 certification as a substitute for FedRAMP. Verify authorization status at marketplace.fedramp.gov and confirm the authorization level matches your data sensitivity requirements.

Executive Order 13526 and Intelligence Community Directive 710 strictly prohibit processing classified national security information on systems not accredited at the appropriate classification level. External commercial LLMs are not accredited for classified processing under any circumstances. Agencies must implement technical controls—not just policy—to prevent classified data from entering any agent pipeline connected to commercial cloud services, and must conduct regular red-team exercises to verify these controls hold.

cta.heading: Automate Your Government Agent Compliance
cta.description: Use Vex to continuously monitor your government research agents against FedRAMP, FISMA, and NIST 800-53 controls—turning this checklist into automated, auditable evidence for your next ATO review.
